The decision rule is explicit: accept the tested configuration only if every
declared corner and transition has guarded setup and hold slack strictly greater
than zero, pulse-width and electrical requirements pass, reset releases
correctly in every domain, and every CDC structure passes its protocol checks.
Otherwise reject the timing claim or narrow its operating boundary. Lab L09
provides the supervised setting for this kind of observation; this chapter does
not invent measurements in advance.

## Sequential design as a timed contract

Feedback makes history available. A latch controls that feedback by level; a
flip-flop narrows system reasoning to repeated edge boundaries. SR, JK, D, and
T descriptions express related next-state requests. Banks and chains of D
flip-flops form parallel and shift registers. Toggle networks form ripple and
synchronous binary counters, while direct or complemented shift-register
feedback forms ring and Johnson counters. FSMs generalize those stored-vector
sequences into explicit, input-dependent behavior.
The abstraction remains trustworthy only when maximum paths meet setup, minimum
paths meet hold, clocks meet waveform and distribution limits, and reset release
is timed.

Asynchronous arrivals cannot satisfy an ordinary edge relationship. A
two-D-flip-flop synchronizer gives the first sampled decision additional time
to resolve and reduces the probability that instability escapes, while
handshakes, Gray transfers, and FIFOs preserve different kinds of information.
That distinction is the practical endpoint: a correct state table, a positive
timing report, and a sensible CDC structure answer different questions, and a
dependable system needs all three to agree with physical evidence.

## Exercises

State active levels, bit order, clock-edge convention, delay bounds, and whether
data are documented, illustrative, simulated, or measured. Estimate before
calculating. Full solutions and assessment variants remain outside the public
book; selected self-checks appear below.

For timing exercises, use the synchronous-path drawing
(@fig-d03-register-path); for CDC exercises, use the synchronizer drawing
(@fig-d03-synchronizer). Copy and annotate the supplied drawing when a prompt
asks for a path or structural repair.

### Quick check

One best answer each; the key follows.

1. An edge-triggered JK flip-flop with $J=K=1$
    a. enters the SR prohibited condition;
    b. toggles once at the active edge;
    c. remains transparent for the active clock level;
    d. always stores one.
2. With bit order $Q_3Q_2Q_1Q_0=1010$ and the chapter's shift convention, a
   serial one accepted into $Q_0$ produces
    a. 1101;
    b. 0101;
    c. 1011;
    d. 0010.
3. During a ripple-counter transition from 0111 to 1000, a decoder
    a. can observe intermediate words while stage delays accumulate;
    b. sees only 0111 and 1000 by definition;
    c. is protected by lowering the decode threshold;
    d. becomes a synchronous counter.
4. With $t_{skew}=\delta_C-\delta_L$, positive skew
    a. helps setup and hurts hold;
    b. hurts setup and helps hold;
    c. helps both setup and hold;
    d. has no effect on either inequality.
5. A two-stage synchronizer
    a. eliminates metastability;
    b. guarantees capture of any pulse width;
    c. reduces the probability that unresolved behavior reaches logic;
    d. makes independently synchronized bus bits coherent.
6. Four changing data bits must cross between unrelated clocks as one word. The
   strongest ordinary choice is
    a. four independent two-flop synchronizers;
    b. one synchronizer on the least significant bit;
    c. a bundled-data handshake or dual-clock FIFO;
    d. an ordinary AND gate on the destination clock.

*[Key: 1-b, 2-b, 3-a, 4-a, 5-c, 6-c.]*

### Retrieval and explanation

1. Define logical state, gated latch, edge-triggered flip-flop, JK and T
   behavior, register, shift register, ripple counter, synchronous counter,
   ring counter, Johnson counter, finite-state machine, clock domain,
   synchronizer, setup time, hold time, and metastability. For each term, name
   the observable signal or relationship that supplies evidence.
2. Explain why `S=R=1` is prohibited for the active-high NOR latch even though
   both gate outputs are definite while the inputs remain asserted.
3. Distinguish a clock enable from gating a clock with ordinary logic.
4. Explain why reset assertion and reset release are separate timing events.
5. Distinguish synchronization, pulse capture, data coherency, and debouncing.

### Storage, register, and counter construction

6. Complete @tbl-d03-characteristic and @tbl-d03-excitation from the four
   characteristic equations. For every $X$, exhibit both permitted Boolean
   choices and verify that an SR choice never becomes $S=R=1$.
7. Convert (a) $J=A\overline Q+B$ and $K=C$ for a JK flip-flop storing $Q$
   and (b) $T_X=E(Q_0+Q_1)$ for a T flip-flop storing $Q_X$ into D-input
   equations. State which values are present-state signals and which are
   external inputs.
8. Starting from 0000, use the SIPO chain in @fig-d03-dff-registers to trace
   eight rising edges for serial sequence 10110100, presenting the leftmost bit
   first on successive edges. Then use @fig-d03-piso-register to parallel-load
   1101, hold $SER_{in}=0$, and read $Q_3$ immediately before
   each of the next four shift edges. Show the four serial-output bits under the
   chapter's declared $Q_0$-to-$Q_3$ shift direction.
9. Draw a four-bit universal register made from D flip-flops and input
   multiplexers. Define non-overlapping codes for hold, parallel load, shift
   toward higher indices, and shift toward lower indices; then state reset,
   load, and enable priority.
10. Derive $T_0$ through $T_3$ and $D_0$ through $D_3$ for a four-bit enabled
    synchronous up-counter. For the corresponding ripple implementation, bound
    the 0111-to-1000 settling interval from four named clock-to-Q maxima plus
    the three interstage routing and polarity-selection maxima, and list every
    intermediate word in @fig-d03-counter-transition-chronogram. Finally,
    derive $D_0$ through $D_3$ for both feedback rules in @fig-d03-ring-johnson-counters
    and verify every state in the shown ring and Johnson cycles.

### State and finite-state reasoning

11. Starting from @eq-d03-sr-relations, verify both stable complementary states
   for $S=R=0$. Then trace set and reset as causal event chains through the two
   gates.
12. Derive @eq-d03-d-latch from a 2-to-1 selection between new data and feedback.
   Evaluate the two enable limits and explain why the Boolean relation says
   nothing about a violation of the required data-stability interval.
13. Extend the acquisition controller with a `CANCEL` input that returns
   `ACQUIRE` to `IDLE` only when `TIMEOUT=0`. State priority among `TIMEOUT`,
   `CANCEL`, and `DONE`; update the state diagram, transition table, and program;
   then rerun the checks.
14. Choose compact binary and one-hot encodings for the controller. List every
   unused code and specify its next state. Compare register bits, decode tests,
   and single-transition bit changes without declaring either encoding
   universally superior.

### Timing derivation and calculation

15. The synchronous-path drawing (@fig-d03-register-path) supplies the
    topology. Re-derive @eq-d03-setup and @eq-d03-hold from its absolute event
    times, marking latest and earliest data paths in different line styles.
    Reverse the skew definition to
    $t'_{skew}=\delta_L-\delta_C$ and show how both signed equations change.
16. A path has $t_{pcq,\max}=0.42$ ns, $t_{pd,\max}=2.30$ ns,
    $t_{su}=0.18$ ns, $t_{skew}=0.10$ ns, and $U_{setup}=0.15$ ns. Estimate and
    calculate the minimum period and strict upper frequency screen. *[Self-check:
    $T_{min}=2.95$ ns and $1/T_{min}\approx339$ MHz; a strict design target must
    be below that unrounded bound and still needs margin.]*
17. For the same path, $t_{ccq,\min}=0.12$ ns,
    $t_{cd,\min}=0.08$ ns, $t_h=0.09$ ns, and $U_{hold}=0.05$ ns. Calculate hold
    slack. Does a slower clock repair the result? *[Self-check:
    $S_{hold}=0.12+0.08-0.09-0.10-0.05=-0.04$ ns; no.]*
18. Recompute the worked HC74–HC157 setup screen for
    $t_{skew}=+3$ ns and $U_{setup}=6$ ns. State why the original hold evidence
    gap remains unchanged.
19. A 20 MHz clock has high duration 18 ns and low duration 32 ns at the
    receiver thresholds. Calculate period, frequency, and duty cycle. Compare
    both pulse widths with a 20 ns strict minimum and state the decision.

### Data interpretation and debugging

20. A timing report shows setup slack $+0.24$ ns and hold slack $-0.06$ ns.
    A proposed repair changes the clock period from 5 ns to 8 ns. Explain which
    result changes and which does not. In the synchronous-path drawing
    (@fig-d03-register-path), annotate the minimum path and propose a repair
    that must be rechecked for setup.
21. A four-bit destination register occasionally reports codes absent from the
    source log during `0111` to `1000`. Each bit has two synchronizer stages.
    Diagnose the structural fault, then draw a repaired handshake or dual-clock
    FIFO interface with source/destination clocks, data, valid/request,
    acknowledge, and reset polarity labeled.
22. An external switch produces five destination events per press. The first
    synchronizer stage never fans out, and its clock is correct. Draw the chain
    from connector to event pulse and mark where input conditioning,
    synchronization, and debouncing belong. State one measurement that
    distinguishes contact bounce from metastability propagation.
23. A controller works after manual reset but sometimes enters an illegal state
    at power-up. List evidence for supply ramp, clock startup, reset assertion,
    reset release, recovery/removal, reset coverage, and state decoding. Do not
    treat “manual reset fixes it” as proof of one cause.

### Visual analysis and repair

24. Copy the data synchronizer and reset-release drawings
    (@fig-d03-synchronizer and @fig-d03-reset-synchronizer-cell). Modify the
    data synchronizer into each of the following flawed
    circuits, then mark the exact rule each flaw violates: (a) combinational
    inversion between `SYNC1` and `SYNC2`; (b) ordinary logic fanout from
    $Q_1$; (c) separate two-stage chains for every bit of a changing binary
    count. Repair each drawing with a structure appropriate to its information,
    then explain why shorting the reset cell's clock bus to its active-low clear
    bus would be a structural fault.
25. Draw aligned `CLK`, `D`, and `Q` waveforms for a positive-edge flip-flop.
    Mark setup, hold, minimum clock-to-Q, and maximum clock-to-Q intervals. Add
    one $D$ transition inside the sampling aperture and draw a family of possible $Q$
    resolution traces rather than one deterministic answer. Label the family
    illustrative, not measured.

### Open design

26. Specify a modulo-10 synchronous counter with enable, synchronous clear,
    terminal-count output, and an illegal-state policy. Provide a state table,
    state diagram, register-plus-next-state block diagram, encoding, setup/hold
    requirements, and at least five independent functional properties.
27. Design a source-to-destination event channel between unrelated clocks. The
    source can produce at most one event per 12 source cycles; the destination
    may pause. Compare pulse stretching, toggle transfer, and a full handshake.
    State the assumptions under which each loses no event, then select one and
    define its reset behavior and acceptance test.
28. Write a timing and CDC review for a two-clock sensor interface carrying a
    12-bit sample word and a `sample_ready` event. Choose bundled-data handshake
    or dual-clock FIFO, state the rate and coherency contract, define reset in
    both domains, and list the evidence needed to accept the implementation.

## Connections

Chapter ID: `D03`. Its direct registry prerequisites are D02 and F07.

- [D02](d02-combinational-logic.qmd) supplies combinational functions,
  propagation and contamination bounds, critical paths, and hazards. D03 places
  those paths between state elements and adds sampling constraints.
- [F07](../01-foundations/f07-capacitors-inductors-transients.qmd) supplies
  physical state, continuity, and transient behavior. D03 abstracts distributed
  physical storage into finite logical state without equating the two.
- [D04](d04-hdl-verification.qmd) expresses edge-triggered state and concurrent
  logic in HDL, then adds assertions, simulation, synthesis, and verification.
- [D05](d05-memory-computer-architecture.qmd) builds memories, datapaths,
  controllers, and processors from registers, FSMs, and cycle timing.
- [D06](d06-fpga-systems.qmd) owns target clock resources, placed-and-routed
  timing closure, FPGA CDC primitives, and system implementation.
- [E02](../04-embedded/e02-microcontrollers-peripherals.qmd) consumes clock,
  reset, register, and peripheral timing at the microcontroller boundary.
- [R02](../06-realization/r02-schematic-pcb.qmd) carries clock, reset, return,
  decoupling, and timed-interface requirements into schematic capture and PCB
  implementation.
- [T01](../appendices/t01-datasheets-standards.qmd) supports interpretation of
  sequential timing tables and their attached test conditions.
- [F06](../01-foundations/f06-measurement-uncertainty-debug.qmd) supplies the
  uncertainty and guarded-decision method required by a physical timing test.
- **Lab L09 — Logic levels, timing, and state** is the direct practical
  companion. It measures the physical signals whose contracts D01 and D03
  develop. Lab L10 belongs downstream with D04 and D06. The
  [practice and project spine](../roadmap.qmd#practice-and-project-spine)
  identifies their course positions.

## References

Claims are cited at their point of use. Storage elements, registers, counters,
FSMs, and synchronous timing follow the canonical digital-design treatment
[@harris2021digital]. Physical timing and synchronization are supported by
Digital Systems Engineering [@dally1998digital]. The worked values and waveform
definitions come from current manufacturer datasheets for the SN74HC74 and
SN74HC157 [@ti2021sn74hc74; @ti2025sn74hc157]. Metastability observations and
the exponential failure-rate treatment use primary literature
[@chaney1973synchronizer; @veendrick1980synchronizers]. Contemporary CDC and
reset implementation guidance is explicitly vendor-scoped
[@amd2025ug574; @amd2026ug949]. The complete
[bibliography](../references.qmd) collects these sources.
